file_name: Feng et al. - 2018 - Raman biophysical markers in skin cancer diagnosis.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used logistic regression classifiers and principal component analysis (PCA) for skin cancer diagnosis tasks (e.g., distinguishing melanoma from dysplastic nevi and nonmelanoma skin cancers from normal tissue). Logistic regression is a machine learning algorithm, and PCA is a statistical dimensionality reduction technique often used in conjunction with ML models for feature extraction and classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving in vivo Raman spectroscopy data collected from 65 patients with 100 lesions. It describes specific methods including an inverse biophysical skin cancer model, logistic regression classifiers, and leave-one-lesion-out cross-validation. The results section reports new diagnostic accuracy metrics (AUCs, sensitivities, specificities) and identifies specific biophysical markers (collagen, triolein, melanin), indicating it is an original research study rather than a review.

title: Raman biophysical markers in skin cancer diagnosis
brief_summary: This study evaluated an inverse biophysical skin cancer model using in vivo Raman spectroscopy to diagnose malignant melanoma (MM) and nonmelanoma skin cancers (NMSC). The researchers analyzed data from 100 lesions in 65 patients, extracting concentrations of eight Raman-active components such as collagen, triolein, and melanin. Logistic regression classifiers with leave-one-lesion-out cross-validation were employed to assess diagnostic performance across four classification tasks. The biophysical model demonstrated diagnostic power comparable to statistical models, with a statistically significant improvement for distinguishing NMSC from normal tissue (AUC 0.76 vs 0.58). Collagen and triolein were identified as the most relevant biomarkers for differentiating MM from dysplastic nevi and NMSC from normal tissue. These findings suggest that Raman spectroscopy can reveal the biophysical basis of skin cancer diagnosis, potentially reducing unnecessary biopsies.

lesion_types_studied.details: 10 lesions from 12 patients included in the dataset; used in classification tasks MM vs DN, [MM, DN] vs norm, and NMSC vs norm.
11 lesions from 17 patients included in the dataset; used in classification tasks MM vs DN, [MM, DN] vs norm, and NMSC vs norm.
14 lesions from 19 patients included in the dataset; grouped with SCC and AK for NMSC analysis; used in classification tasks [BCC, SCC, AK] vs norm and [SCC, BCC] vs AK.
20 lesions from 38 patients included in the dataset; 14 lesions containing both SCC and AK were grouped into SCC; used in classification tasks [BCC, SCC, AK] vs norm and [SCC, BCC] vs AK.
10 lesions from 14 patients included in the dataset; described as a precancerous state; used in classification tasks [BCC, SCC, AK] vs norm and [SCC, BCC] vs AK.
99 adjacent normal tissues from 99 patients included in the dataset; used as the negative class in classification tasks [MM, DN] vs norm and [BCC, SCC, AK] vs norm.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Malignant Melanoma, Dysplastic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, and Normal Skin)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs four binary classification tasks using logistic regression classifiers: (1) Malignant Melanoma (MM) versus Dysplastic Nevus (DN), (2) MM and DN versus normal tissue, (3) Nonmelanoma skin cancer (NMSC: BCC, SCC, AK) versus normal tissue, and (4) SCC and BCC versus AK. Performance was evaluated using ROC curves, AUC, sensitivity, and specificity.

datasets_used.name: Clinical skin cancer screening dataset (University of Texas at Austin / MD Anderson Cancer Center)
datasets_used.description: A prospective clinical dataset of in vivo Raman spectroscopy measurements collected from patients diagnosed with various skin lesions and adjacent normal skin. The study was approved by the Institutional Review Board at the University of Texas at Austin and the University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center (trial registration ID: NCT 00476905). Data was collected using a Raman optical fiber probe integrated in an optical fiber probe-based system with 830-nm excitation.

use.training: Used for training logistic regression classifiers via leave-one-lesion-out cross-validation (LOOCV). In each iteration, one lesion was left out for testing, and the remaining lesions were used for training.
use.validation: Used for validation via leave-one-lesion-out cross-validation (LOOCV). The combination of principal components or biophysical model components yielding the largest Area Under the Curve (AUC) was selected.
use.testing: Used for testing via leave-one-lesion-out cross-validation (LOOCV). Each lesion served as the test set in one iteration.
use.other: Used to extract concentrations of eight Raman-active components (collagen, elastin, keratin, triolein, ceramide, nucleus, melanin, water) using an inverse biophysical skin cancer model.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To build classifiers for distinguishing between different skin lesion types (MM vs DN, MM/DN vs norm, NMSC vs norm, SCC/BCC vs AK) using principal components or biophysical model components as input features.

To evaluate the diagnostic performance of the logistic regression classifiers and to select the optimal combination of input components (PCs or biophysical markers) that yielded the largest Area Under the Curve (AUC).
To reduce the dimensionality of the Raman spectral data by extracting principal components, which were then used as inputs for the logistic regression classifiers.

To statistically compare the Area Under the Curve (AUC) of paired Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves between the statistical model (PCA-based) and the biophysical model to determine if the biophysical model provided equivalent or superior classification potential.

To compare the fit coefficients of the eight biophysical model components between different pathology groups (e.g., DN vs MM, and their adjacent normal tissues) to determine if there were statistically significant differences in component concentrations.
To visualize and quantify the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity for the diagnostic models, and to calculate the Area Under the Curve (AUC) as a performance metric.
To represent the variance of the fit coefficients of the biophysical model components in scatter plots.

hypothesis_testing.test: Bootstrap test for paired ROC curves; Unpaired Student’s t-test for fit coefficients
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: For the bootstrap test: The biophysical model does not provide at least equivalent classification potential compared with the statistical model (i.e., the difference in AUC is not significantly greater than zero or the models are equivalent). For the t-test: There is no statistically significant difference in the fit coefficients (concentrations) of the biophysical model components between the compared pathology groups.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: For the bootstrap test: The biophysical model provides superior classification potential compared with the statistical model (i.e., the AUC of the biophysical model is significantly higher). For the t-test: There is a statistically significant difference in the fit coefficients of the biophysical model components between the compared pathology groups.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: A p-value threshold of 0.05 was used. For the comparison of diagnostic performance, the AUC of the biophysical model for [BCC, SCC, AK] versus normal tissue was found to be statistically significantly better than the statistical model (p < 0.0001). For other classification tasks (MM vs DN, [MM, DN] vs norm, [SCC, BCC] vs AK), the AUCs were not statistically distinguishable (p > 0.05). For biophysical markers, significant differences in component concentrations (e.g., collagen, triolein, melanin) were identified between specific lesion types and normal tissue using unpaired Student’s t-tests (p ≤ 0.05 or p ≤ 0.01).

statistical_model.notes: No statistically significant difference compared to biophysical model (p > 0.05).

biophysical_model.notes: 12 out of 12 MM lesions and 16 out of 17 DN lesions correctly classified. No statistically significant difference compared to statistical model (p > 0.05).

statistical_model.notes: No statistically significant difference compared to biophysical model (p > 0.05).

biophysical_model.notes: No statistically significant difference compared to statistical model (p > 0.05).

statistical_model.notes: Statistically significantly worse than biophysical model (p < 0.0001).

biophysical_model.diagnostically_relevant_components: Collagen, triolein, elastin, nucleus, and ceramide
biophysical_model.notes: Statistically significantly better than statistical model (p < 0.0001).

statistical_model.notes: No statistically significant difference compared to biophysical model (p > 0.05).

input_features: Principal Components (PCs) from Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
Fit coefficients from an inverse biophysical skin cancer model
architecture_details.component_selection: All possible combinations of 1, 2, 3, 4, or 5 components (PCs or biophysical markers) were generated and evaluated. The combination yielding the largest Area Under the Curve (AUC) was selected.

training_details.optimization_criteria: Maximization of Area Under the Curve (AUC)

classification_or_output_head.output: Posterior probabilities for binary classification tasks
tasks: MM and DN versus normal tissue
Nonmelanoma skin cancer (NMSC: BCC, SCC, AK) versus normal tissue
SCC and BCC versus Actinic Keratosis (AK)

preprocessing_techniques: Fifth-order polynomial fitting to remove tissue fluorescence background
Spectral response calibration using a tungsten halogen lamp
Exclusion of spectral band between 800 and 900 cm−1 due to fiber background peak
Removal of sharp room light peak at 1100 cm−1 from five spectra
conclusions: The inverse biophysical skin cancer model captures the diagnostic power of previously used statistical classification models (PCA-based) while providing interpretable skin biophysical composition.

Collagen and triolein were identified as the most relevant biomarkers for distinguishing malignant melanoma (MM) from dysplastic nevi (DN) and for distinguishing nonmelanoma skin cancers (NMSC) from normal tissue.

The biophysical model demonstrated statistically significantly superior diagnostic performance compared to the statistical model for distinguishing NMSC (BCC, SCC, AK) from normal tissue (AUC 0.76 vs. 0.58, p < 0.0001).
For the classification of MM versus DN, the biophysical model achieved an AUC of 0.99, which was not statistically significantly different from the statistical model's AUC of 1.0 (p > 0.05).

For the classification of MM and DN versus normal tissue, the biophysical model achieved an AUC of 0.93, which was not statistically significantly different from the statistical model's AUC of 0.89 (p > 0.05).

For the classification of SCC and BCC versus AK, the biophysical model achieved an AUC of 0.65, which was not statistically significantly different from the statistical model's AUC of 0.62 (p > 0.05).
Raman spectroscopy can reveal the biophysical basis for accurate diagnosis of different skin cancers, potentially leading to a reduction in unnecessary excisional skin biopsies.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, single-center dataset (100 lesions from 65 patients) with significant class imbalance, particularly for malignant melanoma (10 lesions) and actinic keratosis (10 lesions), which limits statistical power and generalizability.

The dataset includes 99 normal tissue samples compared to only 10-20 lesions per cancer type, creating a heavy bias toward normal tissue that may inflate performance metrics for distinguishing cancer from normal but underrepresent cancer subtypes.

External validation was not performed; the study only compares the proposed biophysical model against a previously published statistical model using the same internal dataset, limiting the assessment of independent diagnostic utility.

Diagnostic performance for nonmelanoma skin cancers (NMSC) versus normal tissue was modest (AUC 0.76) with low specificity at high sensitivity (18% specificity at 95% sensitivity), suggesting limited clinical utility for ruling out NMSC in this configuration.
The classification task distinguishing SCC/BCC from AK yielded poor performance (AUC 0.65), indicating the model struggles to differentiate between malignant and precancerous nonmelanoma lesions.

Leave-one-lesion-out cross-validation was used, which is appropriate for small datasets but can result in high variance in performance estimates; no confidence intervals for the AUCs are reported in the results tables.

The biophysical model extracts concentrations of eight components from in vivo spectra using an inverse model fitted to in situ skin data, introducing potential errors if the in situ basis spectra do not accurately represent the in vivo pathological states.

The study acknowledges that tasks (2) and (3) (MM/DN vs norm and NMSC vs norm) are not currently clinically actionable for margin detection but uses normal tissue as a placeholder, which may not reflect real-world clinical scenarios involving other benign lesions.
